steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Cross-Text Connections question. Text 1 states a foundational ecological principle: predators control prey population numbers, supported by a lemming study. Text 2 presents a wolf-Dall sheep study that found no effect, but explicitly acknowledges that many other predation relief studies do show prey increases. The question asks which statement about the principle the author of Text 2 would most likely agree with.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘It has some evidential support, but it should not be regarded as universally applicable.’: Text 2 concedes that "many other predation relief studies show an increase in prey abundance" (evidential support exists) while arguing the principle does not hold for large, slowly reproducing prey like Dall sheep (not universally applicable). This matches the author's position exactly. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘It has been challenged by some studies, but the findings of those studies have not been widely accepted.’: Text 2 does not suggest the challenging studies have been rejected or ignored; in fact, the author treats the contrary evidence as credible. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘It may be true for some predators but only because those predators share certain physical characteristics.’: Text 2 frames the limitation around prey traits (size, reproductive rate), not predator characteristics. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘It is plausible, but many of the studies that support it have methodological flaws.’: Text 2 does not question the methods of supportive studies; it attributes the differing results to prey type, not flawed methodology. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
